topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, almost accusatory. I stood before the canvas, a swirl of indigo and ochre that was unmistakably mine, yet it hung there, framed by stark white borders, positioned next to a collection of works I didn't recognize. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. I walked closer, my fingertips hovering just above the textured surface, a strange knot tightening in my chest. Who had authorized this arrangement? I scanned the room, my gaze darting from the security cameras to the silent, impassive curator. A low murmur escaped my throat, a sound thin and brittle. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice sounding alien in the hushed space. "Where did this come from?"

[story 2]

I traced the edge of the frame with my thumb, the varnish cool beneath my touch. It was my signature technique, my very soul rendered in pigment, displayed as if it belonged to some celebrated master. A slow, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my hand. I took a step back, my shoulders slumping slightly. The air felt thick, charged with an unspoken question. I looked around the room again, my eyes sweeping over the faces of the patrons—a few polite smiles, many blank stares. I couldn't quite form a coherent sentence. I just stared at the painting, a feeling of profound disorientation washing over me, as if the ground beneath my feet had shifted.

[story 3]

"This is mine," I stated, the words coming out firmer than I intended, though my voice cracked on the final syllable. I moved toward the painting, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. When I reached it, I leaned in, studying the brushwork, searching for any sign of alteration, any evidence of intrusion. Nothing. It was exactly as I had left it in my studio. My jaw tightened, and I unconsciously pressed my palms against my temples. A coldness seeped into my bones, a sudden, sharp realization hitting me: this was not a loan, not a collaboration. This was a theft, presented with such casual authority. I straightened up abruptly, turning to face the curator, my expression carefully blank, though my eyes betrayed a frantic need for an answer.
